questions.question_text: Vermont is classified as what type of state regarding real estate closings?
questions.explanation: Vermont is an attorney-closing state. Only licensed attorneys or those with a Vermont title insurance producer license may conduct closings and issue title insurance.

questions.question_text: At a Vermont closing, which professional examines title, prepares documents, and handles disbursement of funds?
questions.explanation: In Vermont, the closing attorney performs the title examination, prepares closing documents, and handles the disbursement of funds. This comprehensive role is required by Vermont's attorney-closing practice.

questions.question_text: A Vermont attorney represents the seller at closing and also wants to issue the buyer's title insurance policy. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, it is a prohibited conflict of interest
distractors: Yes, with written disclosure to both parties
Yes, this is considered standard VT practice
Yes, if both parties provide written consent
questions.explanation: Vermont prohibits an attorney from representing the seller AND providing title insurance to the buyer in the same transaction. This conflict of interest rule protects both parties.

questions.question_text: Besides a licensed attorney, who else may issue title insurance in Vermont?
questions.correct_answer: A VT title insurance producer licensee
distractors: Any licensed real estate broker in VT
Any licensed mortgage originator in VT
questions.explanation: In Vermont, only licensed attorneys or holders of a Vermont title insurance producer license may issue title insurance. Real estate brokers, mortgage originators, and appraisers cannot issue title insurance.

questions.question_text: A Vermont buyer hires an out-of-state title company to handle the closing instead of a local attorney. Is this permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, VT requires a licensed attorney or producer
distractors: Yes, any licensed title company may close in VT
Yes, with written approval from the mortgage lender
Yes, if the company holds a national title license
questions.explanation: Vermont requires that closings be conducted by a licensed Vermont attorney or a Vermont title insurance producer. Out-of-state title companies without proper Vermont licensing cannot handle closings.

questions.question_text: Vermont is classified as what type of state regarding title insurance rates and policy forms?
distractors: Use and file rate and form
questions.explanation: Vermont is a filed rate and filed form state, meaning all title insurance rates and policy forms must be filed with and approved by the Department of Financial Regulation before use.

questions.question_text: In Vermont, who typically issues the title insurance policy to the buyer at closing?
questions.explanation: In Vermont's attorney-closing system, the closing attorney typically issues the title insurance policy. The attorney acts as a title agent for the underwriting title insurance company.

questions.question_text: A Vermont title insurance company wants to charge a cancellation fee when a transaction falls through. Is this permitted?
distractors: Yes, up to 10% of the premium
Yes, if disclosed at the commitment
questions.explanation: Vermont does not permit title insurance companies to charge cancellation or commitment fees. This consumer protection applies even when transactions fail to close.

questions.question_text: Which Vermont state agency approves title insurance rates and forms?
questions.explanation: The Vermont Department of Financial Regulation (DFR) oversees title insurance in the state, approving all rates and policy forms before they may be used in transactions.

questions.question_text: A Vermont buyer's lender requires title insurance. Is an owner's policy also mandatory?
questions.correct_answer: No, the owner's policy is optional
questions.explanation: While most Vermont lenders require a lender's title insurance policy, the owner's policy is optional but recommended. The owner's policy protects the buyer's equity in the property.

questions.question_text: Does Vermont mandate the use of state-promulgated real estate contract forms?
questions.correct_answer: No, promulgated forms are not required
distractors: Yes, for all residential property sales
Yes, for all new construction projects
questions.explanation: Vermont does not mandate state-promulgated contract forms. Vermont Association of Realtors (VAR) standard forms are commonly used in practice but are not legally required.

questions.question_text: Two unmarried co-buyers purchase Vermont property without specifying how they hold title. What form of ownership is presumed?
questions.explanation: In Vermont, tenants in common is the default form of co-ownership when no other form is specified. Joint tenancy requires the four unities, and tenancy by the entirety is limited to married couples.

questions.question_text: Vermont follows which type of recording statute for determining priority of interests in real property?
questions.explanation: Vermont follows a race-notice recording system. To have priority, a subsequent purchaser must both record first AND take without notice of the prior unrecorded interest.

questions.question_text: Where are real estate documents recorded in Vermont?
questions.explanation: Vermont records real estate documents at the town clerk's office, not at the county level. Each town maintains its own land records, which is distinctive to Vermont's municipal governance system.

questions.question_text: A married couple in Vermont purchases a home together. Which form of ownership provides survivorship rights exclusive to married couples?
questions.explanation: Tenancy by the entirety is available only to married couples in Vermont and includes automatic survivorship rights. Vermont also recognizes civil unions for this purpose.
